disagreements.title: Does out-of-home advertising require a significant minimum budget, or can it work for anyone with a scrappy approach?

positions.stance: Out-of-home advertising only makes sense once a company is spending at least ~$5,000/month on performance marketing, with $100k+/month being the threshold where it becomes truly viable. Companies below this threshold should not pursue OOH. Start with a single-market test before scaling.

supporters.evidence: Explicitly stated that if a company is spending less than ~$5,000/month on performance marketing, out-of-home is not a good fit. Framed OOH as a channel that requires sufficient overall marketing budget to be cost-effective, and recommended starting with a single-market test to validate ROI before scaling.

positions.stance: Out-of-home advertising can be executed effectively at very low cost by securing small, high-visibility placements and amplifying them through owned channels. The physical placement is primarily a content creation vehicle; the real reach comes from distributing that content on LinkedIn and other owned channels. Budget is not a barrier.

supporters.guest_context: Host of Exit Five podcast; former CMO
supporters.evidence: Described hiring someone to post album-release-style posters in three strategic locations for under $10k, buying a one-hour Times Square billboard slot, and placing a digital billboard near the Super Bowl venue — all framed as accessible tactics for companies without large budgets. The strategy relies on documenting and distributing the content, not on the media buy's inherent reach.

Explicitly framed the strategy as 'punching above your weight' — purchasing cheap, small versions of premium placements (e.g., regional WSJ ads, affordable Times Square billboards) and amplifying via owned channels. The entire premise is that this works without an enterprise-level budget.

supporters.guest_context: Host of Exit Five podcast; former CMO
supporters.evidence: Described renting the NASDAQ Times Square billboard in 30-minute increments, placing customer testimonial billboards à la carte, and wrapping transit hubs near events — all positioned as cost-effective tactics accessible to companies without large OOH budgets.

disagreements.context_dependency: Partially. Amrita Gurney's advice is explicitly aimed at companies running OOH as a genuine awareness channel with measurable pipeline impact, while the scrappy approach treats OOH primarily as a content creation vehicle amplified through owned channels. However, both camps are addressing the same underlying question — 'should a company with limited budget do OOH?' — and give opposite answers. The disagreement is real even accounting for this nuance, because Gurney's threshold would rule out the very use cases Gerhardt and Ledergor are advocating for.
disagreements.why_it_matters: A B2B marketer with a limited budget deciding whether to invest in OOH will get completely opposite guidance from these experts — one says don't bother until you hit a meaningful spend threshold, while three others say you can make it work for under $10k. Getting this wrong means either wasting budget prematurely or missing a high-leverage brand-building tactic.
